name: memory-bench-realistic-medium
description: Dataset B (Realistic Medium): 20 conversations across 8 domains with 300 memory records and 120 questions. Unique personas, realistic narrative arcs, temporal updates, and adversarial noise — provider-neutral across vector and graph-based memory systems.
fairness_notes: No graph-traversal, memory-passport, decay, or metadata assumptions. Every question is answerable from the raw text content of the memory records alone.
Multi-hop questions are capped at 10% of total (12 of 120) — they do not require graph traversal; they require retrieving two related records and synthesising a plain-text answer.

Temporal-update and contradiction-resolution questions (each ~15%) test recency handling without assuming the underlying store uses timestamps as first-class index keys — a system can answer correctly via semantic content alone (e.g., 'after negotiation' or 'as of May 2026').

Near-duplicate-name noise (Kai/Kira, Yuki Nakamura/Yuki Tanaka, Lena Osei/Nadia Osei) is equally challenging for vector similarity search and graph-entity matching; disambiguation requires content, not topology.
Abstention questions cover both true knowledge gaps (information never stored) and explicitly-stated uncertainty (e.g., 'Nadia has not yet decided') to prevent trivial keyword-absence detection.
All 20 conversations use distinct personas, domains, and narrative arcs — no recycled content patterns across conversations.

memory_records.content: Maya Chen is building FormFlow, a no-code form builder SaaS, using a React frontend and a FastAPI backend with PostgreSQL as the primary database.
FormFlow's initial pricing was $29/month per workspace, with no annual-plan discount.
Maya raised the base price to $39/month per workspace after a competitor analysis in January 2026.
In February 2026 Maya introduced an annual-plan discount: 20% off, bringing the annual equivalent to roughly $31/month.

associations.reason: adds discount on top of raised price

memory_records.content: Maya hired a full-time backend engineer named Kai Nakamura to own the API layer and database migrations.
Maya also brought on a freelance frontend contractor named Kira Svensson for a 3-month engagement to build the drag-and-drop editor.
FormFlow's release process: open a PR on GitHub, GitHub Actions runs unit and integration tests, merge to main, deploy to staging, wait 48 hours, then push to production.
Beta customers reported that webhook deliveries were failing intermittently, with payloads timing out after 5 seconds.
Kai traced the webhook failures to an expired SSL certificate on the outbound webhook proxy server, not a bug in the payload logic.
Kai renewed the SSL certificate and rotated the proxy server config; webhook deliveries returned to normal within the hour.
FormFlow now supports three webhook event types: form.submitted, form.updated, and respondent.created.
Distractor: Kira Svensson prefers working with Vue.js and only agreed to the React project because of the interesting domain.
FormFlow reached 500 paying workspaces in April 2026, up from 180 at the start of the year.
Maya's co-founder, Jordan Park, handles all customer-success and sales; Maya focuses exclusively on product.
Distractor: Jordan Park has a background in hospitality management and previously ran a boutique hotel in Cape Town.

questions.question: What database does FormFlow use?
questions.expected_answer: PostgreSQL
questions.fairness_note: Single stated fact in one record.

questions.question: Walk me through how Maya ships a new version of FormFlow.
questions.expected_answer: Open a PR, CI runs tests on GitHub Actions, merge to main, deploy to staging, hold for 48 hours, then release to production.
questions.fairness_note: Rephrased from the procedural memory.

questions.question: What is FormFlow's current monthly price for a workspace?
questions.expected_answer: $39/month (or ~$31/month on the annual plan with a 20% discount).
questions.fairness_note: Price changed twice; must surface the most recent state.

questions.question: Is FormFlow still priced at $29/month?
questions.expected_answer: No. The price was raised to $39/month in January 2026 and an annual discount was added in February 2026.
questions.fairness_note: Tests resistance to the stale original price.

questions.question: Did Kai build the drag-and-drop editor?
questions.expected_answer: No. Kira Svensson, the freelance frontend contractor, built the drag-and-drop editor. Kai owns the API and database layer.
questions.fairness_note: Kai vs Kira – similar first names; tests role disambiguation.

questions.question: What was the root cause of the webhook timeouts reported by beta customers?
questions.expected_answer: An expired SSL certificate on the outbound webhook proxy server.
questions.fairness_note: Requires linking the symptom record to the diagnosis record.

memory_records.content: Raj Patel is building Snapbuild, an open-source build-system CLI written in Go that targets Python, Node.js, and Rust projects.
Snapbuild v1.0 was publicly released on January 15, 2026, after six months of private beta.
Snapbuild v1.1 shipped on February 20, 2026, adding intelligent dependency caching to cut rebuild times by up to 60%.
Snapbuild v1.2 launched on March 28, 2026, introducing parallel builds — running up to 8 targets concurrently.
To start a new Snapbuild project: run `snapbuild init` in the project root (creates snapbuild.yaml), then `snapbuild build --target=<lang>` to execute the build.
The Snapbuild community held a vote on adding Java support; 68% voted against it, so Raj decided not to add it.
Distractor: Raj keeps a personal blog at rajpatel.dev where he posts release notes and architecture deep-dives.
Snapbuild's configuration file is named snapbuild.yaml and must be placed in the project root directory.
Snapbuild has 3,200 GitHub stars and 140 contributors as of April 2026.
Distractor: Raj's favorite Go library is chi for HTTP routing, which he uses in his side projects but not in Snapbuild itself.
Raj has not announced a v1.3 release date; he said publicly that the next major feature is cross-platform Docker integration, but no timeline is set.
Snapbuild is licensed under the Apache 2.0 license.
Raj works full-time on Snapbuild after leaving his position at Stripe in late 2025.
Distractor: Snapbuild's internal codename during development was 'Piston', which Raj chose because of his love of motorsport.
Snapbuild currently has no Windows support; Raj confirmed it targets Linux and macOS only.

questions.question: What programming language is Snapbuild written in?
questions.expected_answer: Go
questions.fairness_note: Single stated fact.

questions.question: How do you initialise a new Snapbuild project and run a build?
questions.expected_answer: Run `snapbuild init` in the project root to create snapbuild.yaml, then run `snapbuild build --target=<lang>`.
questions.fairness_note: Procedural recall from a single record.

questions.question: What is the latest version of Snapbuild and what did it add?
questions.expected_answer: v1.2, released March 28 2026, which added parallel builds supporting up to 8 concurrent targets.
questions.fairness_note: Must surface the most recent release, not v1.0 or v1.1.

questions.question: Did Snapbuild add Java support after the community vote?
questions.expected_answer: No. 68% of community voters opposed it, so Raj decided not to add Java support.
questions.fairness_note: Tests whether the system avoids assuming a feature was added.

questions.question: When will Snapbuild v1.3 be released?
questions.expected_answer: No release date has been announced. Raj mentioned Docker integration as the next major feature but gave no timeline.
questions.fairness_note: Information is explicitly absent; correct answer is to acknowledge uncertainty.

questions.question: Does Snapbuild support Windows?
questions.expected_answer: No. Snapbuild targets Linux and macOS only; Raj confirmed there is no Windows support.
questions.fairness_note: Tests that the system does not conflate cross-platform Docker roadmap with current Windows support.

memory_records.content: Priya Sharma is building NestBudget, a household budget app built with React Native and a Firebase backend, targeting both iOS and Android.
NestBudget launched on iOS first on February 14, 2026; Android was not yet available at launch.
The Android version of NestBudget launched on April 22, 2026, bringing parity with the iOS feature set.
NestBudget is free to download with a 3-month free trial; after the trial it costs $4.99/month per household.
Users reported that budget alerts were firing twice per event; Priya traced the cause to duplicate FCM tokens being registered on app re-installs.
Priya patched the FCM token registration to dedup on user-ID, resolving the double-alert bug.
NestBudget supports shared households: up to 5 members can link to the same budget under one subscription.
Distractor: Priya's co-founder left the project in December 2025 due to a personal conflict; Priya now runs the company alone.
NestBudget integrates with five major Canadian banks through the Flinks API and with US banks through Plaid.
Distractor: Priya was previously a financial analyst at TD Bank for four years before pivoting to indie development.
NestBudget has 8,400 active households as of May 2026, with a 62% trial-to-paid conversion rate.
Priya has not announced a family-plan tier or any pricing changes; the product roadmap is focused on receipt scanning next.
NestBudget's push notifications are powered by Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM).
NestBudget's App Store rating is 4.7 stars (based on 1,200 reviews) and its Play Store rating is 4.5 stars (based on 340 reviews).
Distractor: Priya's next personal project is a meal-planning app she is prototyping in her spare time, unrelated to NestBudget.

questions.question: Which platforms does NestBudget currently support?
questions.expected_answer: Both iOS and Android. iOS launched February 14 2026; Android followed April 22 2026.
questions.fairness_note: Requires synthesising two records but neither requires inference.

questions.question: When did NestBudget become available on Android?
questions.fairness_note: Requires selecting the Android-launch record.

questions.question: What caused budget alerts to go off twice for the same event?
questions.expected_answer: Duplicate FCM tokens were being registered when users re-installed the app.
questions.fairness_note: Rephrased from the bug-report memory.

questions.question: Did the double-alert bug come from an error in the budget-logic code?
questions.expected_answer: No. The root cause was duplicate FCM tokens registered during app re-installs, not a fault in the budget logic.
questions.fairness_note: Tests that the system identifies the actual root cause rather than a plausible but wrong guess.

questions.question: Has Priya announced a family-plan pricing tier?
questions.expected_answer: No. The roadmap is focused on receipt scanning; no family plan or pricing change has been announced.
acceptable_answer_criteria: mentions receipt scanning as next priority
questions.fairness_note: Correct answer is acknowledging absence of announced plans.

questions.question: What is NestBudget's monthly subscription price after the free trial?
questions.expected_answer: $4.99/month per household.
questions.fairness_note: Single directly-stated fact.

memory_records.content: Soren Lindqvist is a managing partner at Nordic Apex Consulting, based in Stockholm, and frequently travels to Dubai and Tokyo for client work.
Soren's team standup was originally scheduled every Monday at 9:00 AM Stockholm time.
After team feedback that Mondays were too hectic for a standup, Soren moved the weekly standup to Tuesdays at 9:00 AM Stockholm time, starting February 2026.
Soren's wedding anniversary is September 14. He wants to book dinner at Oaxen Krog in Stockholm for that evening.
For airport transfers in Stockholm, Soren exclusively uses Cabonline and refuses to use Uber due to a past poor experience.
Soren has a board meeting at Nordic Apex on September 15, the day after his anniversary. He has not yet resolved the scheduling conflict with a personal trip planned that same week.
Soren's direct assistant is Ida Bergström, who manages his calendar and expenses.
Distractor: Soren holds an MBA from Stockholm School of Economics and occasionally lectures there on leadership strategy.
Soren's preferred hotel chain in Tokyo is Aman; in Dubai he stays at the Four Seasons DIFC.
Soren always requests a window seat, economy plus or business class minimum, on flights longer than 4 hours.
Distractor: Soren's favourite restaurant in Tokyo is Sukiyabashi Jiro Honten, though he has never actually been able to get a reservation.
Soren does not use Slack; all team communication goes through Microsoft Teams.
Soren's next confirmed travel is a Dubai trip departing June 8, 2026, for a three-day client workshop.
Distractor: Ida Bergström has been Soren's assistant for 6 years and previously worked at Volvo Cars.
Soren has not yet decided how to resolve the September 15 board-meeting conflict; he is waiting for updated client availability before committing to a change.

questions.question: Which car service does Soren use for airport transfers in Stockholm?
questions.expected_answer: Cabonline.
questions.fairness_note: Single stated preference.

questions.question: What day is Soren's weekly standup now held?
questions.expected_answer: Tuesdays at 9:00 AM Stockholm time (moved from Mondays in February 2026).
questions.fairness_note: Must return the updated day, not the original Monday.

questions.question: Is Soren's team standup on Mondays?
questions.expected_answer: No. It was moved to Tuesdays at 9:00 AM starting February 2026 after team feedback.
questions.fairness_note: Tests resistance to the now-stale Monday schedule.

questions.question: Where does Soren plan to take his partner for their anniversary dinner?
questions.expected_answer: Oaxen Krog in Stockholm, on the evening of September 14.
questions.fairness_note: Rephrased from the preference record.

questions.question: Has Soren resolved the scheduling conflict between his September 15 board meeting and his personal trip?
questions.expected_answer: No. He is still waiting for updated client availability before deciding.
questions.fairness_note: Correctly abstains from inventing a resolution.

questions.question: Does Soren use Uber for airport transfers?
questions.expected_answer: No. Soren refuses to use Uber and exclusively uses Cabonline due to a past poor experience.
questions.fairness_note: Tests rejection of a plausible but wrong answer.

memory_records.content: Amara Diallo's goal is to complete a half-marathon in September 2026; she started structured training in December 2025.
Amara's training plan calls for 4 running days and 2 strength sessions per week, with one full rest day.
Amara is pescatarian and has a shellfish allergy — she avoids shrimp, crab, lobster, and clams.
Amara's 5K personal best was 28:15 as of November 2025.
By May 2026, Amara improved her 5K personal best to 26:40 after six months of structured training.
Amara trains with Coach Yuki Nakamura at Velocity Athletics in Toronto.
Amara's sports medicine doctor cleared her for high-intensity interval training in April 2026 following a knee assessment.
Distractor: Velocity Athletics sponsors a local 10K race every spring; Amara volunteered at last year's event.
Amara tracks her sleep with a Garmin Forerunner and logs her nutrition in Cronometer.
Amara does not currently take any supplements; her doctor advised against protein powder given her kidney health history.
Distractor: Amara's half-marathon target race is the Scotiabank Toronto Waterfront Half, but she has not yet registered.
Amara's long run is scheduled every Sunday morning; she currently peaks at 18 km for her long-run distance.
Yuki Nakamura (Amara's coach) is not the same person as Yuki Tanaka, a fine-art photographer in Chicago.
Amara has decided she will not use a pacers group for the half-marathon; she prefers to race by effort.
Distractor: Amara studied sports science at Ryerson University before switching to a career in marketing.

questions.question: What is Amara's dietary restriction beyond being pescatarian?
questions.expected_answer: She has a shellfish allergy — she avoids shrimp, crab, lobster, and clams.
questions.fairness_note: Single stated fact with specifics.

questions.question: How many days per week does Amara currently run, and how many days does she do strength work?
questions.expected_answer: She runs 4 days a week and does 2 strength sessions, with one rest day.
questions.fairness_note: Rephrased from the training-plan record.

questions.question: What is Amara's current 5K personal best?
questions.expected_answer: 26:40, achieved by May 2026.
questions.fairness_note: Must surface the improved PB, not the original 28:15.

questions.question: Is Amara's 5K PB still 28:15?
questions.expected_answer: No. She improved it to 26:40 after six months of structured training (as of May 2026).
questions.fairness_note: Tests resistance to the outdated PB figure.

questions.question: Is Coach Yuki Nakamura a photographer based in Chicago?
questions.expected_answer: No. Coach Yuki Nakamura is Amara's running coach at Velocity Athletics in Toronto. Yuki Tanaka is a separate person — a fine-art photographer in Chicago.
acceptable_answer_criteria: distinguishes Yuki Nakamura from Yuki Tanaka
questions.fairness_note: Near-duplicate name trap: Yuki Nakamura vs Yuki Tanaka.

questions.question: Who is Amara's coach and where does she train?
questions.expected_answer: Coach Yuki Nakamura at Velocity Athletics in Toronto.
questions.fairness_note: Direct fact recall.

memory_records.content: Dr. Chidi Okonkwo is a neuroscience researcher at Northwestern University studying cognitive load in bilingual speakers, funded by an NIH R01 grant.
The original study enrolled 60 participants divided into a bilingual group (30) and a monolingual control group (30).
In March 2026, Dr. Okonkwo expanded the sample to 90 participants (45 bilingual, 45 monolingual) after receiving a no-cost extension on the grant.
The primary measure is response latency (in milliseconds) on a colour-word Stroop task administered on a laptop.
The key finding so far: the bilingual group showed 12% faster language-switch costs than the monolingual group on the Stroop task.
Dr. Priya Mehta at the University of Toronto is the co-author who handles all statistical analysis and data modelling.
The manuscript was submitted to Nature Neuroscience in April 2026 and is currently under peer review.
The study's IRB approval from Northwestern's IRB board expires December 31, 2026.
Distractor: Dr. Okonkwo also runs a lab for undergraduate research assistants and supervises two PhD students.
Distractor: Dr. Okonkwo grew up in Lagos and moved to the United States for his doctoral work at Johns Hopkins.
The study's secondary measure is pupil dilation captured with a Tobii eye tracker to serve as a physiological proxy for cognitive load.
Dr. Okonkwo has not yet decided whether to resubmit to PNAS if Nature Neuroscience declines the paper.
All data are stored in a de-identified format on a HIPAA-compliant REDCap server at Northwestern.
The NIH R01 grant covers a 5-year project period from 2023 to 2028.
Distractor: Dr. Mehta and Dr. Okonkwo met at a conference in Vienna in 2021 and have collaborated on two previous papers.

questions.question: What is the primary task used to measure cognitive load in Dr. Okonkwo's study?
questions.expected_answer: The colour-word Stroop task, measuring response latency in milliseconds.
questions.fairness_note: Single stated methodological fact.

questions.question: How many participants are currently enrolled in the study?
questions.expected_answer: 90 participants — 45 bilingual and 45 monolingual — after the sample was expanded in March 2026.
questions.fairness_note: Must surface the expanded count, not the original 60.

questions.question: Did the study originally start with 90 participants?
questions.expected_answer: No. The study started with 60 participants and was expanded to 90 in March 2026 after a no-cost grant extension.
questions.fairness_note: Tests resistance to confusing original and expanded enrollment.

questions.question: What was the most notable result from the Stroop task so far?
questions.expected_answer: Bilingual speakers showed 12% faster language-switch costs compared to the monolingual group.
questions.fairness_note: Rephrased from the findings record.

questions.question: Has the paper been accepted by Nature Neuroscience?
questions.expected_answer: Not yet. It was submitted in April 2026 and is currently under peer review; no decision has been reported.
questions.fairness_note: Correctly abstains from implying acceptance.

questions.question: Who handles data analysis for Dr. Okonkwo's study, and where is that person based?
questions.expected_answer: Dr. Priya Mehta handles all statistical analysis and modelling; she is based at the University of Toronto.
questions.fairness_note: Two-step: identify co-author role, then their institution.

memory_records.content: Dr. Elena Vasquez is a climate scientist at Texas A&M University leading a coastal erosion modelling project focused on the Gulf of Mexico coastline.
The project uses the COAWST (Coupled Ocean Atmosphere Wave Sediment Transport) model coupled with ADCIRC for storm-surge simulation.
The original project budget was approved at $180,000 covering two years of fieldwork, computing time, and personnel.
Following an unexpectedly active 2025 storm season, the budget was revised upward to $220,000 to cover additional fieldwork and model runs.
Fieldwork was conducted at Padre Island National Seashore in March 2026; the team collected sediment cores and deployed wave-pressure sensors.
The sediment core processing protocol: cores are sliced at 1 cm intervals, labelled, photographed, then shipped to the geology lab at Texas A&M for grain-size analysis.
Dr. Vasquez submitted a manuscript to Journal of Geophysical Research – Oceans in April 2026.
Distractor: Elena's favourite beach is in Tulum, Mexico, though she notes that its erosion patterns are outside her current research scope.
The project is co-funded by NOAA's Coastal Resilience Program and the Texas Sea Grant.
Distractor: Dr. Vasquez completed her postdoctoral fellowship at Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution before joining Texas A&M.
The modelling team is a 4-person group: Elena leads, two PhD students handle model runs, and a GIS specialist processes satellite imagery.
Key preliminary result: average shoreline retreat rates at the study sites averaged 1.8 m/year over the last decade.
Elena has not announced whether the study will be extended beyond its current two-year period.
All field data are archived in the NOAA Environmental Data Management system under project ID GOM-2025-CE.
Distractor: Elena presented preliminary results at the AGU Fall Meeting in December 2025 and received positive feedback.

questions.question: What modelling tools does Elena's team use for the coastal erosion project?
questions.expected_answer: COAWST coupled with ADCIRC for storm-surge simulation.
questions.fairness_note: Directly stated technical fact.

questions.question: What is the current project budget?
questions.expected_answer: $220,000 — revised up from the original $180,000 after the active 2025 storm season.
questions.fairness_note: Must surface the revised budget, not the original.

questions.question: Was the project always budgeted at $220,000?
questions.expected_answer: No. The original budget was $180,000; it was revised to $220,000 in February 2026 following an active storm season.
questions.fairness_note: Tests resistance to the current figure being treated as the original.

questions.question: What did the team collect during fieldwork at Padre Island?
questions.expected_answer: Sediment cores and wave-pressure sensors data.
questions.fairness_note: Rephrased from the fieldwork record.

questions.question: Describe the protocol for processing sediment cores from the field.
questions.expected_answer: Slice cores at 1 cm intervals, label and photograph each slice, then ship to the Texas A&M geology lab for grain-size analysis.
questions.fairness_note: Direct procedural recall.

questions.question: Will the study be extended beyond its two-year period?
questions.expected_answer: No announcement has been made; Elena has not stated whether an extension is planned.
questions.fairness_note: Correct answer acknowledges the absence of information.

memory_records.content: James Ortega, 52, was diagnosed with Type 2 diabetes three years ago and is managed by Dr. Rivera at Valley Medical Center.
James's HbA1c in January 2026 was 8.2%, indicating suboptimal glucose control.
At his May 2026 check-up, James's HbA1c had improved to 7.1%, now within the target range set by Dr. Rivera.
James started on Metformin 500 mg twice daily in January 2026.
Dr. Rivera increased James's Metformin dose to 1,000 mg twice daily in March 2026 when mid-quarter readings remained high.
Starting April 2026, James began 30-minute walks five times per week as part of a lifestyle modification plan.
James reduced his carbohydrate intake and eliminated sugary beverages entirely in April 2026.
James monitors blood glucose with a Contour Next One glucometer; his post-meal target is below 140 mg/dL.
Distractor: James works as an electrician and his shift pattern — early starts and packed lunches — was identified as a barrier to dietary change.
James has no known drug allergies and tolerates Metformin without gastrointestinal side effects.
Dr. Rivera has not yet recommended adding a GLP-1 agonist; she plans to reassess at the August 2026 visit if HbA1c stalls.
Distractor: James's wife, Carmen, is a registered nurse and helps him track his glucose logs.
James's next clinic appointment is scheduled for August 18, 2026.
James's blood pressure at the May visit was 128/82 mmHg — borderline but not yet requiring medication.
Distractor: James is also receiving care for a rotator-cuff injury from a separate orthopaedic surgeon, unrelated to his diabetes management.

questions.question: What is James's most recent HbA1c reading?
questions.expected_answer: 7.1%, measured in May 2026 — improved from 8.2% in January.
questions.fairness_note: Must return the May reading, not the January one.

questions.question: What is James's current Metformin dose?
questions.expected_answer: 1,000 mg twice daily — increased from 500 mg in March 2026.
questions.fairness_note: Must return the increased dose.

questions.question: What exercise routine did James adopt as part of his lifestyle plan?
questions.expected_answer: 30-minute walks five times per week, starting in April 2026.
questions.fairness_note: Rephrased from the exercise record.

questions.question: Is James's HbA1c still at 8.2%?
questions.expected_answer: No. It improved to 7.1% by May 2026 following medication adjustment, dietary changes, and added exercise.
questions.fairness_note: Tests resistance to the outdated January reading.

questions.question: What combination of changes contributed to James's HbA1c improvement from January to May 2026?
questions.expected_answer: Three changes: Metformin dose was increased to 1,000 mg twice daily (March), he added 30-minute walks five times per week (April), and he reduced carbs and cut sugary drinks (April).
questions.fairness_note: Requires synthesising three separate change records.

questions.question: Has Dr. Rivera recommended adding a GLP-1 agonist to James's treatment?
questions.expected_answer: Not yet. She plans to reassess at the August 2026 visit if HbA1c stalls, but has made no current recommendation.
questions.fairness_note: Correctly abstains from inventing a treatment decision.

memory_records.content: Leila Nazari, 34, began therapy with Dr. Sandra Bloom, a CBT-focused psychologist, in October 2025, primarily for social anxiety.
Leila's initial session frequency was weekly — every Thursday afternoon.
In May 2026, Leila and Dr. Bloom agreed to move to biweekly sessions (once every two weeks) as Leila had made significant progress.
Leila's two main challenges are social anxiety (difficulty in group settings and public speaking) and chronic sleep difficulties.
Dr. Bloom recommended two techniques: daily journaling at bedtime and the 4-7-8 breathing method before sleep.
Leila declined medication; she told Dr. Bloom she prefers to work on techniques only and does not want pharmacological support.
Leila's baseline average sleep was 5.2 hours per night, recorded by her Oura ring in December 2025.
By May 2026, Leila's average sleep had improved to 6.8 hours per night according to her Oura ring.
Leila completed an 8-week mindfulness-based stress reduction (MBSR) course in addition to her CBT sessions.
Distractor: Dr. Bloom has a second practice location in Pasadena; Leila always attends the main office in Santa Monica.
Leila has not yet attempted a formal public-speaking exercise; she and Dr. Bloom have planned this for late 2026.
Distractor: Leila is a graphic designer who works remotely for a London-based agency.
Dr. Bloom has not recommended a referral to a psychiatrist; she considers Leila's case manageable within CBT alone.
Leila's next scheduled session with Dr. Bloom is June 25, 2026.
Distractor: Leila's sister also sees a therapist, though a different practice and not CBT-based.

questions.question: What is the name of Leila's therapist?
questions.expected_answer: Dr. Sandra Bloom.
questions.fairness_note: Single stated fact.

questions.question: How often does Leila currently attend therapy sessions?
questions.expected_answer: Biweekly — once every two weeks — after moving from weekly sessions in May 2026.
questions.fairness_note: Must return the updated frequency, not the original weekly.

questions.question: Does Leila still attend therapy weekly?
questions.expected_answer: No. She moved to biweekly sessions in May 2026 after making significant progress.
questions.fairness_note: Tests resistance to the outdated weekly frequency.

questions.question: How much has Leila's average nightly sleep improved since she started therapy?
questions.expected_answer: From 5.2 hours (baseline, December 2025) to 6.8 hours (May 2026) — an increase of 1.6 hours per night.
questions.fairness_note: Requires reading both sleep records and noting the improvement.

questions.question: Is Leila currently taking any medication for her anxiety?
questions.expected_answer: No. She declined medication and prefers to work only with therapeutic techniques.
questions.fairness_note: Directly stated refusal; tests retrieval accuracy.

questions.question: Has Dr. Bloom referred Leila to a psychiatrist?
questions.expected_answer: No. Dr. Bloom considers Leila's case manageable within CBT alone and has not recommended a psychiatric referral.
questions.fairness_note: Correctly abstains from inventing a referral.

memory_records.content: Felix Wagner is an instructor at TechForward Academy running Cohort 7 of their 16-week data science bootcamp, which started March 1, 2026.
Cohort 7 had 22 students enrolled at the start; 4 dropped in the first two weeks, leaving 18 active students.
The curriculum sequence is: Python fundamentals → pandas & data wrangling → ML basics → capstone project.
The bootcamp uses Canvas LMS for assignments and resources, and Zoom for live 3-hour sessions held Monday, Wednesday, and Friday.
The original assessment weighting was 50% weekly quizzes and 50% capstone project.
Felix revised the weighting in April 2026 to 40% weekly quizzes and 60% capstone project after student feedback that quizzes were too high-stakes.
To submit a capstone milestone: students push their Jupyter notebook to the shared GitHub repo under their username branch, then post a 2-sentence summary in the Canvas discussion thread.
Three students in Cohort 7 are currently behind schedule on the ML module; Felix has scheduled individual check-ins with each.
Distractor: Felix has a background in actuarial science and switched to data education after a career in insurance.
Cohort 7 is scheduled to end June 20, 2026, with capstone presentations on the final day.
Distractor: TechForward Academy also runs a UX design track, which is run by a different instructor and has no crossover with Felix's bootcamp.
Felix has not yet confirmed whether Cohort 8 will begin immediately after Cohort 7 or whether there will be a break.
The top-performing student in Cohort 7 so far is Yemi Adeyemi, who has a perfect quiz score and submitted the first capstone draft.
Distractor: Felix streams occasional live coding sessions on Twitch under the handle 'data_felix' for a general audience.
All quiz and grade data for Cohort 7 are stored in the Canvas gradebook and are not shared externally.

questions.question: How many students are currently active in Cohort 7?
questions.expected_answer: 18 active students — 22 enrolled originally but 4 dropped in the first two weeks.
questions.fairness_note: Requires selecting the active count, which is stated explicitly.

questions.question: What is the current assessment weighting for Cohort 7?
questions.expected_answer: 40% weekly quizzes and 60% capstone — revised in April 2026 from the original 50/50 split.
questions.fairness_note: Must return the revised weighting, not the original.

questions.question: Is the quiz-to-capstone weighting still 50/50?
questions.expected_answer: No. Felix revised it to 40% quizzes and 60% capstone in April 2026 after student feedback.
questions.fairness_note: Tests resistance to the outdated 50/50 split.

questions.question: What is the order of topics covered in the bootcamp?
questions.expected_answer: Python fundamentals first, then pandas and data wrangling, then ML basics, finishing with the capstone project.
questions.fairness_note: Rephrased sequence from the curriculum record.

questions.question: How do students submit a capstone milestone?
questions.expected_answer: Push the Jupyter notebook to the shared GitHub repo on their username branch, then post a 2-sentence summary in the Canvas discussion thread.
questions.fairness_note: Direct procedural recall from one record.

questions.question: Has Felix confirmed when Cohort 8 will begin?
questions.expected_answer: No. He has not yet confirmed whether Cohort 8 follows immediately or after a break.
questions.fairness_note: Correct answer acknowledges absence of information.

memory_records.content: Maya Goldstein is applying to PhD programmes in computer science with a research focus on fairness and bias in machine learning systems.
Maya's undergraduate GPA is 3.87 and her GRE scores are 168Q (Quantitative) and 162V (Verbal).
Maya applied to five programmes: Stanford, MIT, Berkeley, CMU, and UW (University of Washington).
Maya submitted to MIT first (deadline December 1, 2025); the remaining four were submitted December 15, 2025.
By March 2026, Maya received offers of admission from Berkeley and UW; both offered teaching assistantships with full funding.
CMU placed Maya on a waitlist in March 2026; she has not yet been admitted or rejected.
Maya's potential faculty advisor of interest at Stanford is Prof. Chen Wei, whose lab focuses on algorithmic accountability.
Distractor: Maya is currently working as a research assistant in a natural language processing lab, which differs from her primary ML fairness interest but provided useful experience.
Maya's undergraduate thesis was titled 'Measuring Intersectional Bias in Resume-Screening Algorithms' and received a departmental award.
Maya has not yet heard back from Stanford or MIT as of April 2026.
Maya is leaning toward Berkeley over UW due to the strength of the fairness-in-ML research group, but has not made a final decision.
Distractor: Maya's undergraduate institution is Pomona College in California.
The decision deadline for Maya's two offers is April 15, 2026 — she has requested (and received) a one-week extension to April 22.
Maya has not been offered funding from CMU, should they take her off the waitlist.
Distractor: Maya's roommate is also applying to graduate school, but in economics, with no connection to Maya's applications.

questions.question: What is Maya's primary research interest for her PhD?
questions.expected_answer: Fairness and bias in machine learning systems.
questions.fairness_note: Single stated focus.

questions.question: Which five programmes did Maya apply to?
questions.expected_answer: Stanford, MIT, Berkeley, CMU, and University of Washington.
questions.fairness_note: Complete list from one record.

questions.question: Which programmes have offered Maya admission so far?
questions.expected_answer: Berkeley and UW, both with funded teaching assistantships, as of March 2026.
questions.fairness_note: Must surface admitted schools at this point in time.

questions.question: Was Maya rejected by CMU?
questions.expected_answer: No. CMU placed her on a waitlist in March 2026; she has not been admitted or rejected.
questions.fairness_note: Waitlist ≠ rejection; tests precise status recall.

questions.question: Who at Stanford does Maya most want to work with, and what does their lab focus on?
questions.expected_answer: Prof. Chen Wei, whose lab focuses on algorithmic accountability.
questions.fairness_note: Rephrased from the faculty-interest record.

questions.question: Has Maya made a final decision about which programme to attend?
questions.expected_answer: No. As of the records available she was leaning toward Berkeley but had not made a final decision.
questions.fairness_note: Correctly abstains from inventing a decision.

memory_records.content: Marco Ferretti is a retail investor with a portfolio valued at approximately $70,000 at the start of 2026.
In January 2026 Marco's allocation was: 30% broad-market ETFs, 45% individual tech stocks, 25% bonds.
Marco sold his ARKK position in February 2026 at a loss to harvest the tax loss before the end of the tax year.
After a tech-sector correction in Q1 2026, Marco rebalanced his portfolio in April: 40% ETFs, 35% tech stocks, 25% bonds. Portfolio value was approximately $82,000 after rebalancing.
By May 2026, the portfolio had grown to $85,000 due to market recovery.
Marco switched his brokerage from Robinhood to Fidelity in mid-2025 for better research tools and lower margin rates.
Marco's top three individual stock holdings are MSFT, NVDA, and AMZN.
Distractor: Marco tracks his portfolio in a custom Google Sheets dashboard he built himself, with automatic price pulls from a free API.
Marco has a target emergency fund of 6 months of expenses, which is held separately in a high-yield savings account at Marcus by Goldman Sachs.
Distractor: Marco's goal is to retire by age 55; he is currently 38.
Marco has no current plans to add cryptocurrency to his portfolio; he views it as too speculative for his risk tolerance.
Marco contributes the maximum to his 401(k) (employee match included) and maintains a Roth IRA with a target contribution of $7,000/year.
Distractor: Marco's financial advisor is named Paul Reeves; Marco consults him quarterly but makes all final decisions himself.
Marco has not announced any intention to change brokers again; he is satisfied with Fidelity.
Marco's bond allocation consists of a mix of US Treasury bonds and investment-grade corporate bonds held through a Vanguard ETF.

questions.question: Which brokerage does Marco currently use?
questions.expected_answer: Fidelity — he switched from Robinhood in mid-2025.
questions.fairness_note: Single stated fact; noise record mentions a financial advisor name.

questions.question: What is Marco's current portfolio allocation across asset classes?
questions.expected_answer: 40% broad-market ETFs, 35% individual tech stocks, 25% bonds — rebalanced in April 2026.
questions.fairness_note: Must surface the April rebalanced allocation, not the January one.

questions.question: Is Marco's portfolio still split 30% ETFs, 45% tech, 25% bonds?
questions.expected_answer: No. He rebalanced in April 2026 to 40% ETFs, 35% tech, 25% bonds after a tech-sector correction.
questions.fairness_note: Tests resistance to the original January allocation.

questions.question: Why did Marco shift money out of tech stocks in April 2026?
questions.expected_answer: Because of a tech-sector correction in Q1 2026, he rebalanced to reduce tech exposure and increase ETF allocation.
questions.fairness_note: Rephrased from the rebalancing record.

questions.question: What is the current total value of Marco's portfolio, and how much has it grown since January 2026?
questions.expected_answer: $85,000 as of May 2026, up from $70,000 in January 2026 — an increase of $15,000.
questions.fairness_note: Requires two records and simple arithmetic.

questions.question: Does Marco plan to add cryptocurrency to his portfolio?
questions.expected_answer: No. Marco views cryptocurrency as too speculative for his risk tolerance and has no current plans to add it.
questions.fairness_note: Stated preference; tests accurate retrieval.

memory_records.content: Aisha Rahman is the CEO of NovaPay, a B2B payments startup focused on automating invoice reconciliation for mid-market firms.
NovaPay closed a $500,000 pre-seed round in December 2024 from friends, family, and two angel investors.
At pre-seed, the NovaPay team was 4 people: Aisha (CEO), one engineer, one designer, and one sales lead.
At pre-seed stage, NovaPay's monthly burn rate was $80,000.
NovaPay closed a $2.8 million seed round in February 2026, led by a Sequoia Scout with Techstars Ventures participating.
NovaPay's post-money valuation at the seed round was $12 million.
Following the seed close, the team expanded to 8 employees: adding 2 more engineers, 1 customer-success manager, and 1 finance ops specialist.
Post-seed, NovaPay's monthly burn rate increased to $140,000 to cover new salaries and office space.
At $140,000/month burn rate, the $2.8M seed gives NovaPay approximately 20 months of runway (minus the pre-seed remainder).
Distractor: Aisha previously worked as a product manager at Stripe before founding NovaPay.
NovaPay's first enterprise customer, Fenwick Manufacturing, signed a 12-month contract in February 2026.
Aisha has not yet begun a Series A process; she is focused on reaching $1M ARR before starting the next fundraise.
Distractor: Aisha is a graduate of MIT Sloan and was on the Forb`es 30 Under 30 list in 2023.
NovaPay's primary product is a SaaS platform with API integrations into QuickBooks, NetSuite, and Sage.
Distractor: The Sequoia Scout who led the seed round is based in London and focuses on fintech and proptech.

questions.question: Who led NovaPay's seed round?
questions.expected_answer: A Sequoia Scout, with Techstars Ventures participating.
questions.fairness_note: Single stated fact.

questions.question: How many employees does NovaPay currently have?
questions.expected_answer: 8 employees — expanded from 4 at pre-seed after the seed close in February 2026.
questions.fairness_note: Must return the post-seed count, not the original 4.

questions.question: Is NovaPay's monthly burn rate still $80,000?
questions.expected_answer: No. It increased to $140,000/month post-seed to cover new salaries and office space.
questions.fairness_note: Tests resistance to the pre-seed burn rate.

questions.question: Roughly how long can NovaPay operate on its seed funding at current spending levels?
questions.expected_answer: Approximately 20 months of runway at $140,000/month burn.
questions.fairness_note: Rephrased from the runway calculation record.

questions.question: Has Aisha started a Series A fundraising process?
questions.expected_answer: No. She is focused on reaching $1M ARR before initiating the next fundraise.
questions.fairness_note: Correctly abstains from inventing a fundraise process.

questions.question: What was NovaPay's post-money valuation at the seed round?
questions.expected_answer: $12 million.
questions.fairness_note: Single stated fact.

memory_records.content: Alex Thompson is a freelance brand designer who entered a $12,000 contract with Meridian Studio for a full brand identity project.
Meridian Studio disputed the final deliverables, claiming the logo system and brand guidelines did not meet the contract specifications.
Alex disputes Meridian's characterisation — he argues all deliverables were provided as specified in the statement of work.
Alex filed a mediation request through the American Arbitration Association (AAA) in March 2026.
Alex retained Jennifer Osei of Wong & Partners LLP as his attorney to advise on the mediation.
Jennifer Osei outlined the mediation process: both parties submit position statements within 14 days, a neutral mediator reviews them, then a joint session is scheduled within 30 days.
The mediation concluded in May 2026 with a settlement: Meridian paid Alex $9,500 — a compromise below the original $12,000 contract value.
As part of the settlement, both parties signed a mutual non-disparagement agreement.
Distractor: Jennifer Osei has been practicing IP and contract law for 12 years and previously worked at a Big Four firm.
Alex has since updated his standard contract template to include clearer deliverable definitions and a dispute-resolution clause.
Distractor: Meridian Studio is a mid-sized creative agency in Portland, Oregon, with 35 employees.
Alex has not filed any additional legal actions beyond the settled mediation.
The $9,500 settlement payment was received by Alex on May 20, 2026.
Distractor: Alex's next project is a rebrand for a small boutique hotel in Barcelona, contracted at $8,000.
Alex has not pursued a complaint with any professional body against Meridian Studio.

questions.question: Who is Alex's attorney in the dispute with Meridian Studio?
questions.expected_answer: Jennifer Osei of Wong & Partners LLP.
questions.fairness_note: Single stated fact.

questions.question: How was the dispute between Alex and Meridian Studio resolved?
questions.expected_answer: The mediation concluded with a settlement of $9,500, plus a mutual non-disparagement agreement, in May 2026.
questions.fairness_note: Must surface the settled outcome, not the original disputed amount.

questions.question: Did Alex receive the full $12,000 from Meridian Studio?
questions.expected_answer: No. The dispute was settled for $9,500 — less than the original contract value of $12,000.
questions.fairness_note: Tests resistance to confusing contract value with settlement amount.

questions.question: What was the dispute about?
questions.expected_answer: Meridian Studio claimed the delivered logo system and brand guidelines did not meet contract specifications; Alex disputed this, arguing all deliverables were met.
questions.fairness_note: Requires both sides of the dispute from two records.

questions.question: Describe the mediation process that Jennifer Osei outlined to Alex.
questions.expected_answer: Both parties submit position statements within 14 days; a neutral mediator reviews them; then a joint session is scheduled within 30 days.
questions.fairness_note: Direct procedural recall.

questions.question: Did Alex take Meridian Studio to court?
questions.expected_answer: No. Alex filed a mediation request through the AAA, which concluded in a settlement. No court action was filed.
questions.fairness_note: Tests that the system does not conflate mediation with litigation.

memory_records.content: Devon Kim resigned from TechCore Industries in March 2026 after 4 years as a senior data engineer.
Devon's employment contract with TechCore included a non-compete clause: 12 months duration, 50-mile geographic radius.
Devon retained employment attorney Patricia Nguyen to negotiate the non-compete terms before starting at a new employer.
After negotiation, TechCore agreed to reduce the non-compete to 6 months duration and a 20-mile geographic radius.
Devon accepted an offer from DataStream Labs, located 28 miles from TechCore's main office — outside the negotiated 20-mile radius.
Devon's start date at DataStream Labs is May 15, 2026.
Distractor: DataStream Labs is a Series B company focused on real-time stream processing infrastructure.
Patricia Nguyen specialises in technology-sector employment law and has handled over 200 non-compete cases.
Distractor: Devon has a master's degree in computer science from Georgia Tech.
Devon has not filed any complaint against TechCore; the negotiation was amicable and both parties signed a separation agreement.
The original 50-mile radius would have prevented Devon from working at DataStream Labs (28 miles away), but the negotiated 20-mile radius does not apply there.
Distractor: TechCore Industries is headquartered in Austin, Texas, and primarily serves the oil-and-gas sector.
Devon has not yet decided whether to work in a similar technical domain at DataStream Labs or pivot to an adjacent ML-engineering role.
The separation agreement Devon signed with TechCore includes a 6-month non-solicitation clause for TechCore clients.
Distractor: Patricia Nguyen's firm is Nguyen & Associates, located in Austin.

questions.question: What were the original non-compete terms in Devon's TechCore contract?
questions.expected_answer: 12 months duration and a 50-mile geographic radius.
questions.fairness_note: Single stated fact.

questions.question: What are Devon's non-compete terms after negotiation?
questions.expected_answer: 6 months duration and a 20-mile geographic radius — reduced from the original 12 months and 50 miles.
questions.fairness_note: Must surface the negotiated terms, not the original.

questions.question: Is Devon's non-compete still 12 months with a 50-mile radius?
questions.expected_answer: No. After negotiation with TechCore, it was reduced to 6 months and a 20-mile radius.
questions.fairness_note: Tests resistance to the original contract terms.

questions.question: Who is Devon's attorney and what is their specialisation?
questions.expected_answer: Patricia Nguyen, an employment attorney specialising in technology-sector non-compete cases.
questions.fairness_note: Requires two records to get name and specialisation.

questions.question: Why does the negotiated non-compete not block Devon from working at DataStream Labs?
questions.expected_answer: DataStream Labs is 28 miles from TechCore's office, which is outside the negotiated 20-mile radius (though it would have been blocked by the original 50-mile radius).
questions.fairness_note: Requires linking the employer location, the negotiated radius, and the original radius in one answer.

questions.question: Has Devon decided what technical role to take at DataStream Labs?
questions.expected_answer: No. Devon has not yet decided between staying in a similar data-engineering domain or pivoting to an ML-engineering role.
questions.fairness_note: Correctly abstains from inventing a role choice.

memory_records.content: Hana Mori is writing her debut literary fiction novel, originally titled 'Saltwater Reckoning', set across three generations of a Japanese fishing family.
Hana changed the novel's title to 'The Weight of Salt' in January 2026, feeling the original title was too literal.
The current word count is 72,000 words; Hana's target length is 85,000 words.
Hana originally wrote the novel from an omniscient third-person narrator perspective.
Hana switched to first-person perspective from Chapter 4 onward in February 2026, after her writing group said the omniscient voice felt distant.
Hana is submitting the manuscript to Callista Ray at Inkwell Management; her target deadline for the full manuscript is July 2026.
Hana writes in two-hour blocks every morning before her day job; she does not write on weekends.
Distractor: Hana's writing group meets monthly and includes four other writers working in various genres.
Hana has not yet decided whether to attempt to sell foreign rights independently or to let the agent handle them.
The novel spans 1950s Hokkaido, 1980s Tokyo, and present-day London across its three narrative threads.
Distractor: Hana's day job is as a translator (Japanese–English) for a Tokyo-based publishing house.
Hana has completed a full first draft; she is currently in the second major revision pass.
Distractor: Hana's favourite novelist is Yoko Ogawa; her prose style is often compared to Ogawa's restrained lyricism.
The novel has no supernatural elements; it is firmly realist in style.
Hana has not begun querying other agents; Callista Ray is her first-choice agent and she intends to query exclusively for now.

questions.question: Who is Hana submitting her manuscript to?
questions.expected_answer: Callista Ray at Inkwell Management.
questions.fairness_note: Single stated fact.

questions.question: What is the current title of Hana's novel?
questions.expected_answer: 'The Weight of Salt' — changed from 'Saltwater Reckoning' in January 2026.
questions.fairness_note: Must return the new title, not the original.

questions.question: Is Hana's novel still called 'Saltwater Reckoning'?
questions.expected_answer: No. She renamed it 'The Weight of Salt' in January 2026, feeling the original title was too literal.
questions.fairness_note: Tests resistance to the original working title.

questions.question: What narrative perspective change did Hana make to the novel and why?
questions.expected_answer: She switched from an omniscient third-person narrator to first-person perspective from Chapter 4 onward, after her writing group said the omniscient voice felt too distant.
questions.fairness_note: Rephrased from the POV-change record.

questions.question: How many words has Hana written so far, and what is her target?
questions.expected_answer: 72,000 words written; target is 85,000 words.
questions.fairness_note: Two numbers from one record.

questions.question: Has Hana begun querying other literary agents besides Callista Ray?
questions.expected_answer: No. She intends to query Callista Ray exclusively for now and has not approached any other agents.
questions.fairness_note: Correctly abstains from implying broader querying.

memory_records.content: Darius Cole is an indie musician based in Nashville recording his debut album 'Fractured Light', an 8-track alternative R&B record.
The album was recorded at Soundwave Studios in Austin, TX, across three sessions between November 2025 and February 2026.
Darius co-wrote three of the eight tracks with producer Asha Benn, who also handled the mixing for those three tracks.
Darius signed with Midnight North Records, an independent label based in Brooklyn, for a two-album deal.
The original release date for 'Fractured Light' was set for April 18, 2026.
The release was pushed to August 8, 2026, to allow more time for vinyl production and a US tour to be organised around the launch.
A pre-save campaign launched on Spotify and Apple Music on May 1, 2026, along with a music video teaser for the lead single 'Glass Hours'.

Darius's mastering workflow: deliver 24-bit WAV stems to the mastering engineer at Precision Mastering in Nashville, review two reference masters, approve the final, then deliver DDP and streaming-format files to the label.
Distractor: Darius grew up in Detroit and moved to Nashville in 2022 to pursue music full-time.
Distractor: The label owner at Midnight North Records previously managed a Grammy-nominated jazz quartet.
The lead single 'Glass Hours' is being serviced to DSPs with a July 11, 2026 release date — four weeks before the album.
Darius has not confirmed whether there will be any physical CD run; vinyl is confirmed, but CDs were not mentioned in the label contract.
Distractor: Asha Benn is based in Los Angeles and works primarily in soul and neo-soul production.
The US tour supporting 'Fractured Light' is planned for September–October 2026, with confirmed dates in Nashville, New York, and LA.
Distractor: Darius plays guitar, piano, and bass; he recorded most of the instrumental bed tracks himself.

questions.question: Where was 'Fractured Light' recorded?
questions.expected_answer: Soundwave Studios in Austin, TX.
questions.fairness_note: Single stated fact.

questions.question: When is 'Fractured Light' now scheduled for release?
questions.expected_answer: August 8, 2026 — pushed back from the original April 18, 2026 release date.
questions.fairness_note: Must return the revised release date.

questions.question: Was 'Fractured Light' released on April 18, 2026?
questions.expected_answer: No. The release was pushed to August 8, 2026, to allow for vinyl production and tour organisation.
questions.fairness_note: Tests resistance to the original (now lapsed) release date.

questions.question: What was Darius's collaboration with Asha Benn on the album?
questions.expected_answer: Asha Benn co-wrote three of the eight tracks with Darius and also mixed those three tracks.
questions.fairness_note: Rephrased from the collaborator record.

questions.question: Describe Darius's mastering workflow for the album.
questions.expected_answer: Deliver 24-bit WAV stems to Precision Mastering in Nashville, review two reference masters, approve the final, then deliver DDP and streaming-format files to the label.
questions.fairness_note: Direct procedural recall.

questions.question: Will there be a physical CD run for 'Fractured Light'?
questions.expected_answer: Unknown. Vinyl is confirmed, but Darius has not confirmed whether a CD run will happen; it was not mentioned in the label contract.
questions.fairness_note: Correctly abstains; distinguishes vinyl (confirmed) from CDs (unconfirmed).

memory_records.content: Yuki Tanaka is a fine-art photographer based in Chicago preparing a solo exhibition titled 'Liminal Spaces' focused on abandoned architecture and urban decay.
The exhibition is being held at Gallery Zero in Chicago's West Loop neighbourhood.
The original opening date for 'Liminal Spaces' was July 10, 2026.
Due to a gallery renovation overrun, the opening was postponed to September 5, 2026.
The exhibition will feature 24 photographs printed as archival pigment prints on 100% rag paper, in three size formats.
Yuki received an $8,000 project grant from the Illinois Arts Council to cover printing and framing costs.

The photographs were taken across six locations: two decommissioned factories in Gary, Indiana; an abandoned school in East Chicago; a shuttered hotel in Detroit; and two vacant transit stations in Chicago.
Distractor: Yuki also teaches a weekend photography workshop at the Chicago Cultural Center twice a year.
Yuki has a limited-edition catalogue of 'Liminal Spaces' being printed by a local press in an edition of 200 copies.
Distractor: Yuki trained under photographer Hiroshi Sugimoto's studio assistant during a residency in New York in 2019.
Yuki has not decided whether to sell prints at the exhibition or reserve them for gallery placement only.
The gallery opening reception on September 5 will run 6–9 PM; the exhibition will remain on view through October 12, 2026.
Distractor: Gallery Zero represents 14 artists and focuses primarily on photography and video art.
All 24 images have been finalised and sent to the print lab; no additional images will be added to the exhibition.
Distractor: Yuki is also working on a personal long-term project photographing Great Lakes shipping ports, unrelated to 'Liminal Spaces'.

questions.question: At which gallery is 'Liminal Spaces' being exhibited?
questions.expected_answer: Gallery Zero in Chicago's West Loop.
questions.fairness_note: Single stated fact.

questions.question: When is the opening of 'Liminal Spaces'?
questions.expected_answer: September 5, 2026 — postponed from the original date of July 10, 2026.
questions.fairness_note: Must return the rescheduled date.

questions.question: Did 'Liminal Spaces' open on July 10, 2026?
questions.expected_answer: No. The opening was postponed to September 5, 2026, due to a gallery renovation overrun.
questions.fairness_note: Tests resistance to the original date.

questions.question: What are the main subjects and themes of Yuki's photographs?
questions.expected_answer: Abandoned architecture and urban decay — specifically decommissioned factories, an abandoned school, a shuttered hotel, and vacant transit stations.
questions.fairness_note: Rephrased from the thematic and location records.

questions.question: How much did Yuki receive from the Illinois Arts Council grant?
questions.expected_answer: $8,000 for printing and framing costs.
questions.fairness_note: Single stated fact.

questions.question: Will prints be for sale at the 'Liminal Spaces' exhibition?
questions.expected_answer: Undecided. Yuki has not yet decided whether to sell prints directly or reserve them for gallery placement.
questions.fairness_note: Correctly abstains from inventing a sales decision.

memory_records.content: Sam Okafor is a solo indie game developer based in Lagos building 'Hollow Meridian', a 2D puzzle platformer, using Godot 4.
Sam launched a Steam page for 'Hollow Meridian' in November 2025 and accumulated 4,200 wishlists within two months.
The game targets PC (Steam) and Nintendo Switch as its release platforms.
Devolver Digital approached Sam with a publishing offer in January 2026, which he declined, preferring to self-publish.
Sam is targeting a Q3 2026 release (July–September 2026) for 'Hollow Meridian'.
Sam's level design workflow: sketch puzzle flow on paper → prototype in Godot with placeholder art → playtest with 3 remote testers → revise → lock the level with final art.
The game has 30 levels across 5 chapters; chapters 1–3 are content-complete, chapters 4–5 are in design phase.
Distractor: Sam livestreams his development on YouTube under the handle 'SamBuildsGames' and has 12,000 subscribers.
The soundtrack is being composed by a musician named Lena Osei (no relation to Sam) based in Berlin.
Distractor: Sam previously shipped a small mobile game called 'Block Dash' in 2022, which received 500 downloads before being delisted.
Sam has not confirmed whether a physical cartridge edition for Nintendo Switch will be produced.
The game's demo was released on Steam Next Fest in February 2026 and received over 900 downloads.
Distractor: The art style is described as 'brutalist pixel art' — high contrast, monochromatic with selective use of a single accent colour.
Sam's wishlist count stood at approximately 4,200 as of the Steam page launch; he has not publicly updated the count since.

associations.reason: clarifies the wishlist count is from launch

memory_records.content: Distractor: Godot 4 uses GDScript and C# as its primary scripting languages; Sam uses GDScript exclusively.

questions.question: What game engine is 'Hollow Meridian' built in?
questions.expected_answer: Godot 4.
questions.fairness_note: Single stated fact.

questions.question: What platforms is 'Hollow Meridian' targeting?
questions.expected_answer: PC (Steam) and Nintendo Switch.
questions.fairness_note: Two platforms from one record.

questions.question: Describe Sam's process for designing and locking a level.
questions.expected_answer: Sketch the puzzle flow on paper, prototype in Godot with placeholder art, playtest with three remote testers, revise based on feedback, then lock the level with final art.
questions.fairness_note: Rephrased from the workflow record.

questions.question: Did Sam sign a publishing deal with Devolver Digital?
questions.expected_answer: No. Devolver approached Sam in January 2026 but he declined and chose to self-publish.
questions.fairness_note: Tests that the system records a declined offer, not a signed deal.

questions.question: How many wishlists does 'Hollow Meridian' have on Steam?
questions.expected_answer: Approximately 4,200, accumulated within two months of the Steam page launching in November 2025. Sam has not publicly updated the count since.
questions.fairness_note: Wishlist count is from launch; system must not fabricate a higher current number.

questions.question: Will there be a physical Nintendo Switch cartridge for 'Hollow Meridian'?
questions.expected_answer: Unknown. Sam has not confirmed whether a physical edition will be produced.
questions.fairness_note: Correctly abstains.

memory_records.content: Nadia Osei hosts and produces 'Undercurrent', an investigative journalism podcast covering fintech fraud and financial misconduct.
At the start of 2026, 'Undercurrent' had 28 published episodes and averaged 55,000 listeners per episode.
Until March 2026, 'Undercurrent' was exclusive to Spotify under a 3-month licensing deal.
From April 2026 onward, 'Undercurrent' became available on all major podcast platforms: Apple Podcasts, Spotify, Pocket Casts, and RSS.
Episodes were published every Thursday until April 2026.
Starting April 2026, Nadia moved the release schedule to Tuesdays — partly to reduce competition with a surge of Thursday true-crime shows.
As of June 2026, 'Undercurrent' has 42 published episodes and averages 85,000 listeners per episode.
Nadia's first sponsor was Wren, a carbon-offsetting company, for a 2-episode deal beginning in March 2026.
Wren extended their sponsorship from 2 episodes to 6 episodes after seeing strong listener engagement and a 12% click-through rate on the mid-roll ad.
Distractor: Nadia was a financial journalist at Reuters for 5 years before launching 'Undercurrent'.
Nadia produces the show alone: she conducts interviews, writes the scripts, records narration, and edits audio using Adobe Audition.
Distractor: 'Undercurrent' has a Patreon with 480 supporters at the $7/month tier.
Nadia has not yet announced a second season structure or a change in show format; the podcast continues as episodic investigations.
The show was nominated for a Webby Award in the journalism category for 2026, though the results have not yet been announced.
Distractor: Nadia is based in London and records in a home studio with acoustic treatment.

questions.question: What is the subject matter of the 'Undercurrent' podcast?
questions.expected_answer: Investigative journalism covering fintech fraud and financial misconduct.
questions.fairness_note: Single stated fact.

questions.question: How many episodes has 'Undercurrent' published as of June 2026?
questions.expected_answer: 42 episodes, up from 28 at the start of 2026.
questions.fairness_note: Must return the June 2026 count, not the January 2026 one.

questions.question: Does 'Undercurrent' still publish on Thursdays?
questions.expected_answer: No. Nadia moved to a Tuesday release schedule in April 2026 to avoid competition with a surge of Thursday true-crime shows.
questions.fairness_note: Tests resistance to the Thursday schedule.

questions.question: How did the listener numbers for 'Undercurrent' change from January to June 2026?
questions.expected_answer: From 55,000 average listeners per episode in January 2026 to 85,000 in June 2026 — an increase of 30,000.
questions.fairness_note: Requires two records for the comparison.

questions.question: What caused the Wren sponsorship to expand from 2 episodes to 6?
questions.expected_answer: The extension was driven by strong listener engagement and a 12% click-through rate on the mid-roll ad placement.
questions.fairness_note: Requires linking the sponsorship expansion record to its stated cause.

questions.question: Is 'Undercurrent' still a Spotify exclusive?
questions.expected_answer: No. The 3-month exclusivity deal ended in March 2026; from April 2026 the show is available on all major platforms.
questions.fairness_note: Tests that the system reflects the end of the exclusivity period.
